mappings.rationaleAI: Core coverage of magnetic phenomena, magnetic materials (dia/para/ferro/antiferro/ferrimagnetism), atomic magnetism, and the relationship between electricity and magnetism (C.1-C.5).
Directly covers Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (B.3.a), the development of field theory (D), and the foundational link between electricity and magnetism (C.2).
Explains the electromagnetic nature of light, wave-particle duality of the photon (E.2.b), and quantum theory of radiation including Einstein's photoelectric effect (E.2.a).
Treats electromagnetic radiation, wave propagation, diffraction, and interference -- phenomena central to the electromagnetic spectrum (E.1.c) and transmission of radiation (E.3.b).
Addresses the quantum theory of radiation (E.2), including Planck's and Einstein's contributions to the photoelectric and Compton effects (E.2.a) and wave-particle duality of the photon (E.2.b).
Directly relevant to relativistic electrodynamics (F) and the four-dimensional formulation of electromagnetic theory (F.1).
Explores electrical conduction mechanisms in metals (B.2.b), magnetic properties of superconducting materials (C.5), and electromagnetic effects in condensed matter.
Examines conduction of electricity in metals and semiconductors (B.2.b), magnetic phase transitions in solids (C.5), and superconductivity as an electromagnetic phenomenon.

Traces the historical development of ideas about electricity, magnetism, and the nature of light (E.1.a), from Newton's corpuscular theory through Faraday's field concept to Maxwell's equations and Einstein's photon.
Provides broad coverage of electrostatics, electrodynamics, electromagnetic radiation, and the relationship between force, matter, and electromagnetic fields (A-E).
Details the principles of generation and transmission of AC single- and multiphase power (B.3.k), electricity grids, and practical electromagnetic induction applications.
Addresses solar photovoltaics rooted in the photoelectric effect (B.2.d/E.2.a), battery electrochemistry (B.5.d), and electricity grids for AC power transmission (B.3.k).
Directly relevant to electricity in the atmosphere (A.1.e), including lightning, atmospheric electric phenomena, and the electromagnetic spectrum.
Probes electromagnetic fields in vacuum, Maxwell's unification of electricity and magnetism, and the nature of electromagnetic radiation in free space (E.3.b, D).
Discusses nuclear magnetic moments and magnetic resonance (C.3.d), electron behavior, and quantum mechanical foundations of electromagnetic interactions.
Treats nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (C.3.d), electrolysis and electro-osmosis (B.5.d), and electron behavior in chemical systems relevant to electrical conduction.
Directly relevant to thermoelectric effects (B.2.c) where electric energy transforms into thermal energy, and resistance heating (B.5.c).
Addresses SI units and standardization relevant to definitions of electric quantities (B.1.a) and measurement of electrostatic forces and fields (A.4).
Examines the Sun's magnetic field, sunspot cycles, and solar wind, directly relevant to terrestrial magnetism (C.5.c.vi) and electricity in the atmosphere (A.1.e).
Explains Earth's dynamo-generated magnetic field and solar wind interactions, directly relevant to terrestrial magnetism (C.5.c.vi).
Analyzes chemical bonding, crystal lattice structures, and material properties relevant to dielectrics (A.2), magnetic materials (C.5), and piezoelectricity (B.5.a).
